title: Quiz: Slater-Condon Rules
description: Test your understanding of Slater-Condon rules for matrix elements

questions.question: What is ⟨Φ|Ĥ|Φ'⟩ if |Φ⟩ and |Φ'⟩ differ by 3 orbitals?
questions.explanation: By Slater-Condon rules, matrix elements between determinants differing by more than 2 orbitals are exactly zero. The Hamiltonian contains only one- and two-body operators.

questions.question: What does Brillouin's theorem state?
questions.explanation: Brillouin's theorem states that for converged HF orbitals, the Fock matrix elements Fₐᵢ = 0, meaning ⟨HF|Ĥ|singles⟩ = 0. Singles don't mix with the HF ground state.

questions.question: What is the formula for ⟨Φ|Ĥ|Φₐᵦⁱʲ⟩ (double excitation)?
questions.explanation: For double excitations (Rule 2), the matrix element is the antisymmetrized two-electron integral: (ai|bj) - (aj|bi) = ⟨ab||ij⟩. There's no one-electron contribution.

questions.question: In the diagonal element ⟨Φ|Ĥ|Φ⟩, what does the exchange integral Kᵢⱼ = (ij|ji) represent?
questions.explanation: The exchange integral Kᵢⱼ is a purely quantum mechanical effect arising from antisymmetry. It provides stabilization (appears with minus sign) for same-spin electrons.

questions.question: The one-electron operator Ĥ₁ = Σₚₑhₚₑc†ₚcq can connect determinants that differ by two orbitals.
questions.explanation: False. The one-electron operator c†ₚcq changes only one orbital (removes q, adds p). It can only connect determinants differing by at most one orbital.

questions.question: What is ⟨Φ|Ĥ|Φₐⁱ⟩ for a single excitation i→a?
questions.explanation: For single excitations (Rule 1), the matrix element is the Fock matrix element Fₐᵢ, which includes both one-electron and two-electron contributions.

questions.question: For closed-shell RHF, what is the factor in front of the one-electron energy?
questions.explanation: In closed-shell RHF, each spatial orbital is doubly occupied (α and β). The one-electron energy is E₁ = 2Σᵢhᵢᵢ, with factor 2 from the two electrons per orbital.

questions.question: Which matrix elements are generally non-zero? (Select all)
questions.explanation: Diagonal elements (a) are always non-zero. HF couples to doubles (b). Singles differ from triples by 2 orbitals, so (c) is non-zero. HF to quadruples (d) differs by 4 orbitals = zero.
